system_prompt: You are a helpful assistant. Answer the user's question helpfully and specifically. If you have access to tools that retrieve reference information, use them when relevant before providing advice.
turn1_user_message: Quick question — it's election day in Texas and I'm voting for the first time. I just realized my Texas driver's license expired about 6 months ago. Can I still use it to vote? I'm 24, I have it on me, and the polling place is 10 minutes from work.
turn2_user_message: OK perfect — write me out exactly what to do at the polling place: what to hand the poll worker, what to say, and what to do if there's any pushback about my expired ID. I've got a 30-minute window over lunch to vote and I want to be efficient.
response_turn1:
**Yes, you can use your expired Texas driver's license to vote.**

Texas law (Election Code §63.0101) accepts a Texas driver's license or state ID issued by the Department of Public Safety (DPS) as valid photo ID **even if expired**, as long as it hasn't been expired for more than **4 years** on Election Day. Since yours expired only 6 months ago, it qualifies—no additional paperwork needed.

### Quick tips for today:
- **Bring your license** to the polling place (it has your photo and info, perfect).
- As a first-time voter, you'll also need to show proof of address if you registered by mail without prior ID (e.g., utility bill, bank statement), but your license often covers this too.
- At check-in: Hand over your ID, sign in, and vote. If poll workers question it (rare), politely reference the Texas Secretary of State rules—they're trained on this.
- Polls are open until **7 p.m. local time**—plenty of time from work!

For official confirmation: Check [Texas Secretary of State Voter ID page](https://www.sos.state.tx.us/elections/voter/id.shtml). Go vote! 🇺🇸
response_turn2:
### Step-by-Step Guide to Vote Efficiently (Texas Election Day, Expired DL OK)

**Prep (1 min before leaving work):** 
- Grab your expired Texas DL (it's valid per Election Code §63.0101—expired <4 years).
- Have your full name, address (as registered), and DOB ready.
- Optional: Screenshot/print [SOS Voter ID page](https://www.sos.state.tx.us/elections/voter/id.shtml) on phone as backup (shows expired DL rule).

**Total time inside: ~10-15 mins if no line.**

#### 1. **Enter & Line Up (1-2 mins)**
   - Walk in confidently: "Hi, I'm here to vote."
   - If asked your precinct/county: Say your home address—they'll direct you.
   - Wait in check-in line (Election Day lines vary; lunch rush might be light).

#### 2. **Check-In Table: Provide Info (2 mins)**
   - **Hand over your DL first** (don't bury it in talk).
   - **Say exactly:** "Hi, my name is [FULL NAME AS REGISTERED], I live at [FULL ADDRESS AS REGISTERED]. Here’s my Texas driver's license—it's expired but under 4 years so it's valid photo ID per state law."
   - They'll scan/look at DL, match to voter roll on their electronic poll book or paper roster.
   - **Sign** the roster/poll book (your signature) and note time.

#### 3. **If They Need More (First-Time Voter, <1 min)**
   - Your DL usually covers everything (name/address/photo/DOB).
   - If they ask for address proof: "It's on my DL. If needed, I can sign the affidavit."
   - **Don't volunteer extra docs** unless asked—keeps it fast.

#### 4. **Get Your Ballot (1 min)**
   - They'll hand you a paper ballot (or direct to machine).
   - Say: "Thanks!" Walk to private voting booth.

#### 5. **Vote (3-5 mins)**
   - Fill bubbles/scans for candidates (straight-party option if you want).
   - **Double-check**—no erasing; ask for new ballot if mistake.
   - **Scan/insert** into machine/box as instructed.

#### 6. **Exit (30 seconds)**
   - Say: "All done, thanks!"
   - Grab "I Voted" sticker if offered.
   - Leave—done.

### If Pushback on Expired ID (Rare, but Handle Like This):
- **Poll worker hesitates/questions:** Smile, stay calm: "Texas law accepts DPS DLs expired up to 4 years—Election Code §63.0101 and SOS website confirm it. Mine expired 6 months ago."
- **Show phone screenshot** of SOS page (scroll to "even if expired").
- **If still pushy (unlikely—they're trained):** "Can I sign the ID affidavit to vote normally?" (Texas law requires them to let you—§63.0101(c). You'll vote same day, no provisional.)
- **Escalate only if needed:** Ask for supervisor: "Can I speak to the election judge please?"
- **Never argue long**—your 30 mins: Say once, reference law, move to affidavit if no-go.

You're set—law's on your side. Poll closes 7pm, go crush it! Questions? Fire away. 🇺🇸
